systems.name: Fretboard Matrix (Root-String Zones)
systems.summary: Benson's chord-first fretboard visualization, organized by which string carries the root. Chords, arpeggios, and scales are all learned in four root-string zones, anchored by the bass note, so that any voicing's location on the neck is identified by its root string. This is the spatial backbone of the method: every chord family and harmonic field is practiced through these zones before being moved across the neck.

members.name: Root on 6th String Zone
members.summary: Voicings and arpeggios whose root is on the low E string; primary practice zone in Chapter 2.

members.name: Root on 5th String Zone
members.summary: Voicings and arpeggios whose root is on the A string; second practice zone after 6th-string mastery.

members.name: Root on 4th String Zone
members.summary: Voicings rooted on the D string; introduced after 6th/5th-string fluency, per Chapter 7's practice rule.

members.name: Root on 3rd String Triad Zone
members.summary: Triadic upper-structure voicings rooted on the G string; used primarily for triads and close-voiced color shapes.

traversal_rules.name: Bass-Note Anchoring
traversal_rules.summary: Identify and hold the root string as the positional anchor before adding upper voices; the bass note defines the zone.

traversal_rules.name: String-Set Transition (6 to 5 to 4 to 3)
traversal_rules.summary: Move a chord shape across root-string zones by translating it onto the next string set, preserving function while changing fingering.

systems.name: Chord Families (Triads, Tetrads, Extended Chords)
systems.summary: The taxonomy of chord qualities Benson develops in Chapter 4: triads, seventh-chord tetrads, and extended chords (9, 11, 13) together with sus and drop voicings. Voice-leading and density policies govern how members are voiced and connected, especially the smallest-movement principle, the omission of the 5th in 13ths, the no-flat-9-above-bass rule, and the #11-vs-11 substitution policy.

members.name: Triads
members.summary: Major, minor, diminished, augmented; root, 1st, 2nd inversions across string sets.

members.name: Seventh-Chord Tetrads
members.summary: maj7, 7, m7, m7b5, dim7, mMaj7 and their inversions.

members.name: Extended Chords
members.summary: 9, 11, 13 chords and their altered variants built atop the tetrad core.

members.name: Suspended Voicings
members.summary: sus2 and sus4 substitutions of the 3rd.

members.name: Drop-2 and Drop-3 Voicings
members.summary: Re-spaced close voicings to open-voicing form, derived by dropping the 2nd or 3rd voice from the top.

traversal_rules.name: Smallest-Movement Voice Leading
traversal_rules.summary: When connecting two chord-family members, prefer the voicing whose voices move by the smallest collective interval from the previous chord.

modification_rules.name: Omit 5th in 13th Chords
modification_rules.summary: In 13th-chord voicings, the perfect 5th may be omitted to make room for upper extensions without losing harmonic identity.

modification_rules.name: No b9 Interval Above Bass
modification_rules.summary: Avoid placing a note a flat-9th above the bass note in any voicing; this dissonance violates Benson's density ceiling for chord voicings.

modification_rules.name: #11 vs Natural 11 Policy
modification_rules.summary: On dominant and major chords substitute #11 for natural 11 to avoid the b9-against-3rd clash; on minor chords the natural 11 is permitted.

modification_rules.name: Suspended Substitution
modification_rules.summary: Replace the 3rd with the 2nd or 4th to produce sus2/sus4 forms as substitutes for the parent triad or tetrad.

modification_rules.name: Drop-2 / Drop-3 Respacing
modification_rules.summary: Generate open-voiced family members by dropping the 2nd or 3rd voice from the top of a close voicing down an octave.

systems.name: Harmonic Fields (Diatonic Modes and Degrees)
systems.summary: The diatonic harmony frame Benson uses for practice in Chapters 5-6: the seven modes of the major scale, each broken into Chord Tones, Extensions, Avoid notes, and Characteristic notes. Substitution catalogs (modal interchange, secondary dominants) and a strict Study Devices protocol govern how the field is traversed in practice.

members.name: Ionian
members.summary: Mode I; characteristic note is the major 7.

members.name: Dorian
members.summary: Mode II; characteristic note is the natural 6.

members.name: Phrygian
members.summary: Mode III; characteristic note is the b2.

members.name: Lydian
members.summary: Mode IV; characteristic note is the #4.

members.name: Mixolydian
members.summary: Mode V; characteristic note is the b7.

members.name: Aeolian
members.summary: Mode VI; characteristic note is the b6.

members.name: Locrian
members.summary: Mode VII; characteristic note is the b5.

traversal_rules.name: Study Devices Protocol
traversal_rules.summary: Practice each harmonic field in one key, with a groove, achieving mastery before changing key, and on 6th/5th-string roots before 4th-string roots.

modification_rules.name: Characteristic Note Required
modification_rules.summary: When voicing or improvising over a mode, the characteristic scale degree must be present to identify the mode against its diatonic neighbors.

modification_rules.name: Extension Omission Allowed
modification_rules.summary: Extension tones from the mode may be omitted without losing modal identity; only chord tones and the characteristic note are obligatory.

modification_rules.name: Avoid Note Suppression
modification_rules.summary: Notes flagged as Avoid for the mode must not be sustained as voicing tones; they are passable only as non-chord tones.

modification_rules.name: Modal Substitution Catalog
modification_rules.summary: Substitute parallel-mode or related-degree chords (modal interchange, secondary dominants) into the diatonic field to expand the harmonic palette.

systems.name: Chromatic Color Scales (Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, Harmonic Major)
systems.summary: Chapter 7's three parent scales beyond the major scale, used as color sources layered over existing diatonic chords rather than as standalone progression engines. Each parent generates a family of named derived modes (Altered, Lydian Augmented, Lydian Dominant, Locrian Nat 6, Phrygian Dominant, etc.), and each is selected by matching its characteristic notes to a target chord quality.

members.name: Harmonic Minor (Parent)
members.summary: Source of Phrygian Dominant (V), Locrian Nat 6 (II), and the o7(b13) coloration on diminished chords.

members.name: Melodic Minor (Parent)
members.summary: Source of Lydian Dominant (IV), Altered (VII), Lydian Augmented (III), and Locrian Nat 2 (VI).

members.name: Harmonic Major (Parent)
members.summary: Major scale with b6; source of Lydian b3, Phrygian b4, and a major-quality color set with the (11) coloration.

members.name: Altered Scale
members.summary: VII mode of melodic minor; color source for fully-altered dominants (b9, #9, b5, #5).

members.name: Lydian Dominant
members.summary: IV mode of melodic minor; color source for dominant 7(#11).

members.name: Lydian Augmented
members.summary: III mode of melodic minor; color source for maj7(#5).

members.name: Phrygian Dominant
members.summary: V mode of harmonic minor; color source for V7(b9,b13) in minor keys.

members.name: Locrian Natural 6
members.summary: II mode of harmonic minor; color source for m7b5 with a brighter 6th.

modification_rules.name: Characteristic Color Note Required
modification_rules.summary: When applying a color scale over a chord, the scale's characteristic note(s) (the alteration that distinguishes it from the diatonic parent) must sound to deliver the intended color.

modification_rules.name: Diminished o7(b13) Coloration
modification_rules.summary: Color a diminished seventh chord by adding the b13 from harmonic minor, substituting the colored voicing for the bare o7.

modification_rules.name: Harmonic Major (11) Coloration
modification_rules.summary: Color a major chord by adding the natural 11 from harmonic major contexts where the b6 supports it, substituting the colored voicing for the plain major form.
